revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24221752933|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[ar:إيواكورا تومومي]][[ca:Iwakura Tomomi]][[cs:Tomomi Iwakura]][[de:Iwakura Tomomi]][[en:Iwakura Tomomi]][[es:Iwakura Tomomi]][[fa:ایواکورا تومومی]][[fr:Iwakura Tomomi]][[id:Iwakura Tomomi]][[it:Iwakura Tomomi]][[ja:岩倉具視]][[ko:이와쿠라 도모미]][[nl:Iwakura Tomomi]][[pl:Tomomi Iwakura]][[pt:Iwakura Tomomi]][[ru:Ивакура Томоми]][[sr:Ивакура Томоми]][[sv:Iwakura Tomomi]][[th:อิวากูระ โทโมมิ]][[tr:Iwakura Tomomi]][[uk:Івакура Томомі]][[vi:Iwakura Tomomi]][[zh-classical:岩倉具視]][[zh:岩倉具視]] {{Short description|Japanese politician (1825–1883)}} {{family name hatnote|Iwakura|lang=Japanese}} {{Multiple issues| {{more citations|date=November 2024}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2019}} {{expand Japanese|topic=bio|date=November 2024}} }} {{Infobox person | honorific_suffix = [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] |name = Iwakura Tomomi
岩倉 具視 |image = File:IWAKURA Tomomi.jpg |caption = |birth_date = {{birth date|1825|10|26}} |birth_place = {{ill|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|1=Kyoto}} |death_date = {{death date|1883|7|20}} |death_place = {{ill|Tokyo|qid=Q7473516|1=Tokyo Proper}} |death_cause = |resting_place ={{ill|Kaian-ji|qid=Q11558954|1=Kaian-ji Temple}} |known_for = Leading{{ref label|reference_name_A|a|}} the [[:en:Meiji Revolution|Meiji Revolution]] Heading the {{ill|Iwakura Mission|en|Iwakura Mission|qid=Q696860|lt=Iwakura Mission|1=Iwakura Mission}} |education = |occupation = {{ill|diplomat|qid=Q193391|1=Diplomat}}; {{ill|politician|qid=Q82955|1=Politician}} |gender = {{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} |nationality = {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} |title = {{ill|Chamberlain of Japan|qid=Q2948088|1=Chamberlain of Japan}}; {{ill|Dainagon|qid=Q1354706|1=Dainagon}}; {{ill|Udaijin|qid=Q1351174|1=Minister of the Right}}; {{ill|gijō|qid=Q4138017|1=gijō}} |spouse = {{ill|Iwakura Makiko|qid=Q11474172|1=Iwakura Makiko}} |children = {{ill|Iwakura Michitomo|qid=Q40031270|1=Iwakura Michitomo}}; {{ill|Iwakura Tomosada|qid=Q11266398|1=Iwakura Tomosada}}; {{ill|Iwakura Tomotsune|qid=Q22127007|1=Iwakura Tomotsune}}; {{ill|Mori Hiroko|qid=Q11539444|1=Mori Hiroko}}; {{ill|Q113163640|qid=Q113163640|1=Q113163640}}; {{ill|Toda Kiwako|qid=Q20041660|1=Toda Kiwako}} |father = {{ill|Yasushika Horikawa|qid=Q11427581|1=Yasushika Horikawa}} |family = {{ill|Horikawa|qid=Q11427578|1=Horikawa}}; {{ill|Iwakura|qid=Q11474158|1=Iwakura family}} |awards = {{ill|Grand Cordon of the Supreme Order of the Chrysanthemum|qid=Q5361043|1=Grand Cordon of the Supreme Order of the Chrysanthemum}}; {{ill|Order of the Rising Sun, 1st class|qid=Q10901470|1=Order of the Rising Sun, 1st class}} |native_name = 岩倉具視 |given_name = {{ill|Tomomi|qid=Q4460317|1=Tomomi}} }} {{nihongo|'''Iwakura Tomomi'''|岩倉 具視||extra= October 26, 1825{{snd}}July 20, 1883}} was a Japanese {{ill|Politician|en|Politician|qid=Q82955|lt=statesman|1=Politician}} during the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|qid=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}} and {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}.Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric. (2005). "Iwakura Tomomi" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 408|page=408}}. He was one of the leading figures of the [[Meiji Restoration]], which saw Japan's transition from feudalism to modernism. Born to a noble family, he was adopted by the influential Iwakura family. By 1858 he was an advisor to {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|qid=Q299179|lt=Emperor Kōmei|1=Emperor Kōmei}}, but was exiled from the royal court from 1862 to 1867 for his moderation. After release, he became the liaison between the court and the {{ill|Shishi (Japan)|en|Shishi (Japan)|qid=Q2279517|lt=anti-Tokugawa movement|1=Shishi (Japan)}}. He played a central role in the new {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|qid=Q1322181|lt=Meiji government|1=Government of Meiji Japan}} after 1868. He successfully opposed aggressive policies in Korea in {{ill|Seikanron|en|Seikanron|qid=Q370290|lt=the crisis of 1873|1=Seikanron}}, and was nearly assassinated by his enemies. He led the 50-member {{ill|Iwakura Mission|en|Iwakura Mission|qid=Q696860|lt=Iwakura Mission|1=Iwakura Mission}} for 18 months in Europe and America, studying modern institutions, technology, and diplomacy. The Mission promoted many key reforms that quickly modernized Japan. He promoted a strong imperial system along Western lines, and played a central role in creating financial institutions for the nation. The {{ill|500 yen note|en|500 yen note|qid=Q11372556|lt=500 Yen banknote|1=500 yen note}} issued by the {{ill|Bank of Japan|en|Bank of Japan|qid=Q333101|lt=Bank of Japan|1=Bank of Japan}} carried his portrait. ==Early life== Iwakura was born in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}},{{cite EB1911 |wstitle=Iwakura, Tomomi |volume=15 |page=101}} the second son of low-Ranking courtier and [[kuge|nobleman]], {{nihongo|[[Horikawa Yasuchika]]|堀川 康親}}. Through his mother, he was a first cousin of {{ill|Emperor Ninkō|en|Emperor Ninkō|qid=Q312351|lt=Emperor Ninkō|1=Emperor Ninkō}} and descended from the 10th-century {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|qid=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}}.{{Cite EB1911|wstitle=Iwakura, Tomomi|volume=15|page=101}} His childhood name was Kanemaru but fellow court nobles called him Iwakichi (typically thought of as a commoner's name){{cn|date=July 2021}}. Confucian scholar [[Fusehara Nobuharu]] taught him {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|qid=Q9581|lt=Confucianism|1=Confucianism}} from childhood and suggested that {{nihongo|[[Iwakura Tomoyasu]]|岩倉 具康}} adopt Kanemaru as his child.{{Cite book|last=Sasaki|first=Suguru|title=Iwakura Tomomi [岩倉具視]|publisher=Yoshikawa Hirohumi kan|year=2006|location=Tokyo}} Tomoyasu did this In 1836, giving Kanemaru his own family name. In 1838, the boy changed his name from Kanemaru to Tomomi. In ''{{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|qid=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}}'', most of the 137 court nobles had a long tradition. In contrast, the Iwakura house had only become independent from the Koga house in the early Edo period.{{Cite book|last=Sakamoto|first=Kazuto|title=Iwakura Tomomi[岩倉具視：幕末維新期の調停者]|publisher=Yamagawa shuppansha|year=2018|location=Tokyo}} This newness gave the Iwakura house lower status. It did not have a family business, so they had little money. Iwakura once showed his daring and disregard for the old customs of the nobility by making his home available as a gambling house in order to earn money. In 1839, when he was 14 years old, he had a coming-of-age ceremony and started to get involved in politics. He was trained by the ''[[:en:Kampaku|Kampaku]]'' {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|en|Takatsukasa Masamichi|qid=Q594626|lt=Takatsukasa Masamichi|1=Takatsukasa Masamichi}} and wrote the opinion for the imperial Court reformation. In 1854 he became a [[:en:Chamberlain of Japan|chamberlain]] to {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|qid=Q299179|lt=Emperor Kōmei|1=Emperor Kōmei}}, his first cousin once removed. ==As court noble== As with most other courtiers in Kyoto, Iwakura opposed the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]'s plans to end Japan's {{ill|Sakoku|en|Sakoku|qid=Q332075|lt=national isolation policy|1=Sakoku}} and to open Japan to foreign countries. When {{ill|Hotta Masayoshi|en|Hotta Masayoshi|qid=Q910241|lt=Hotta Masayoshi|1=Hotta Masayoshi}}, a ''[[Rōjū]]'' of the Tokugawa government came to Kyoto to obtain imperial permission to sign the {{ill|Treaty of Amity and Commerce (United States–Japan)|en|Treaty of Amity and Commerce (United States–Japan)|qid=Q1194699|lt=Treaty of Amity and Commerce (United States–Japan)|1=Treaty of Amity and Commerce (United States–Japan)}} in 1858, Iwakura gathered courtiers who opposed the treaty and attempted to hinder negotiations between the Shōgun and the Court. The imperial court and shogunate had a conflict because of the {{ill|Treaty of Amity and Commerce (United States–Japan)|en|Treaty of Amity and Commerce (United States–Japan)|qid=Q1194699|lt=Treaty of Amity and Commerce|1=Treaty of Amity and Commerce (United States–Japan)}}. The shogunate agreed to sign the treaty, but almost the people of the imperial court had the opposite idea. However, [[Hisatada Kujō|Kujō Hisatada]], an authority of the imperial court, supported the shogunate's idea. Iwakura wanted to prevent the treaty of Amity and Commerce, so he decided to use an unusual method. In particular, he planned to have a direct talk with the ''[[Sesshō and Kampaku|Kanpaku]]'' by leading a lot of nobles. As a result of this, the ''Kanpaku'' agreed to prevent signing the treaty. This incident is called the demo of eighty-eight retainers of the Imperial court, and it made Iwakura famous. Two days later of the incident, Iwakura wrote a political written opinion. It was said that he wanted to postpone signing the treaty because he thought Japan needed to learn about the situation and customs of foreign countries. Also, he emphasized domestic consensus in the written opinion. In 1858, Ii Naosuke became Tairō and signed the Treaty of Amity and Commerce without asking the emperor Kōmei. This angered the emperor, and the relationships between the imperial court and the shogunate became worse. Iwakura made an effort to find a way to settle peacefully. After ''{{ill|Tairō|en|Tairō|qid=Q956844|lt=Tairō|1=Tairō}}'' {{ill|Ii Naosuke|en|Ii Naosuke|qid=Q716940|lt=Ii Naosuke|1=Ii Naosuke}} was assassinated in 1860, Iwakura supported the ''Kobugattai Movement'', an alliance of the Court and the Shogunate. The central policy of this alliance was the marriage of the Shōgun [[Tokugawa Iemochi]] and Princess [[:en:Kazu-no-Miya Chikako|Kazu-no-Miya Chikako]], the younger sister of the Emperor Kōmei. Andō and Kuze became ''[[rōjū]]'' and began to have power, and they suggested the marriage for the ''{{ill|Kōbu gattai|en|Kōbu gattai|qid=Q1189374|lt=Kōbu gattai|1=Kōbu gattai}}'', the settlement of the imperial court and the shogunate. Although the shogunate ordered marriage of Kazunomiya and [[Tokugawa Iemochi|Iemochi]], the {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|qid=Q299179|lt=Emperor Kōmei|1=Emperor Kōmei}} opposed to this request because Kazunomiya and [[Arisugawanomiya]] had been already engaged. Also, he could not understand about ''Kōbu gattai''. At that time, the emperor Kōmei demanded an opinion of Iwakura, because he knew that Iwakura was a talented person. Iwakura suggested that the emperor should let the shogunate promise the executing of the treaty withdrawal and allow the marriage of Kazunomiya and Iemochi. The emperor accepted Iwakura's opinion, and the marriage of Kazunomiya and Iemochi was officially decided and the shogunate declared to execute ''jōi''. ''Samurai'' and nobles who supported the more radical ''[[Sonnō jōi]]'' policy saw Iwakura as a supporter of the Shogunate, and put pressure on the Court to expel him. As a result, Iwakura left the Court in 1862 and moved to Iwakura, north of Kyoto. Emperor Kōmei claimed that Iwakura was innocent, but could not stop people who supported radical ''[[Sonnō jōi]].'' Iwakura decided to hide upon receiving a threatening message from {{ill|Takechi Hanpeita|en|Takechi Hanpeita|qid=Q1074298|lt=Takechi Hanpeita|1=Takechi Hanpeita}} from Tosa clans that if Iwakura did not leave the urban area of Kyoto, he would be exposed to Shijōkawara, which meant his death. He started to live in the Reigenji-temple in Nishigamo, where a grave of Iwakura ancestor was. On that day, he wrote about his despair in his diary. After that he found that the Reigenji-temple was not suitable for a long-term stay, so he moved to the {{ill|Saihō-ji (Kyoto)|en|Saihō-ji (Kyoto)|qid=Q1516753|lt=Saihōji-temple|1=Saihō-ji (Kyoto)}}. Moreover, the imperial court issued the order of expulsion from the city, so he had to move to Iwakura village. Sanshirō, a foster parent of Iwakura's third son, suggested this place. ==In exile== The exile continued for around five years. During exile, he had some connections with people who were familiar with politics. In Iwakura he wrote many opinions and sent them to the Court or his political companions in {{ill|Satsuma Domain|en|Satsuma Domain|qid=Q676187|lt=Satsuma Domain|1=Satsuma Domain}}. For example, in 1865, Iwakura showed '''Sōrimeichū'''(叢裡鳴虫), a written opinion, to {{ill|Ōkubo Toshimichi|en|Ōkubo Toshimichi|qid=Q356118|lt=Ōkubo Toshimichi|1=Ōkubo Toshimichi}} and {{ill|Komatsu Kiyokado|en|Komatsu Kiyokado|qid=Q703847|lt=Komatsu Tatewaki|1=Komatsu Kiyokado}} from {{ill|Satsuma Domain|en|Satsuma Domain|qid=Q676187|lt=Satsuma clans|1=Satsuma Domain}}. He used this title to compare himself to an insect in the grasses. Moreover, he sent '''Zoku：Sōrimeichū'''(続・叢裡鳴虫) to Ōkubo and Komatsu. He claimed the importance of national unity for external crises. He relied on Satsuma clans because he found that they were capable people. Also, people from Satsuma clans had connections with the imperial court, so Iwakura tried to get them to send his written opinions to the imperial court. Responses of {{ill|Nijō Nariyuki|en|Nijō Nariyuki|qid=Q3073063|lt=Nijō Nariyuki|1=Nijō Nariyuki}}, a ''Kanpaku'', and Satsuma clans were subtle. Iwakura was away from politics because of the penalty, so there was a difference between the Iwakura's thoughts and the actual political situation. In 1866 when Shōgun Iemochi died, Iwakura attempted to have the Court seize political initiative. He tried to gather ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' under the name of the Court but failed. When the Emperor Kōmei died the next year, there was a rumor Iwakura had plotted to murder the emperor with poison, but he escaped arrest. After the {{ill|Kinmon incident|en|Kinmon incident|qid=Q139871|lt=Kinmon incident|1=Kinmon incident}}, he returned to normal life, and his innocence was proved. On October 15th, 1867, the shogunate, [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] returned his power to the imperial court. Yoshinobu worried about the external crisis and recognized the importance of national unity. In this situation, {{ill|Ōkubo Toshimichi|en|Ōkubo Toshimichi|qid=Q356118|lt=Ōkubo|1=Ōkubo Toshimichi}} planned the coup that abolished the shogunate and ''[[Sesshō and Kampaku|sekkan]]'' systems. On December 8th, Iwakura participated in the ''kogosho'' meeting and talked about the disposal of Yoshinobu. During this meeting, {{ill|Yamauchi Toyoshige|en|Yamauchi Toyoshige|qid=Q711001|lt=Yamauchi Yōdō|1=Yamauchi Toyoshige}} shouted, claiming that they should let Yoshinobu participate in this meeting and criticized a coup. It is a famous episode that Iwakura scolded Yamauchi because his attitude was rude, but this episode is a fiction. In fact, Iwakura could not easily convince Yamauchi, and their discussion was continued for the long term. Finally, Iwakura and Ōkubo convinced those who opposed the disposal of Yoshinobu. With {{ill|Ōkubo Toshimichi|en|Ōkubo Toshimichi|qid=Q356118|lt=Ōkubo Toshimichi|1=Ōkubo Toshimichi}} and [[Saigō Takamori]], on January 3, 1868, he engineered the seizure of the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]] by forces loyal to Satsuma and Chōshū, thus initiating the [[Meiji Restoration]]. He commissioned Imperial banners with the sun and moon on a red field, which helped ensure that the encounters of the Meiji Restoration were generally bloodless affairs. ==Meiji bureaucrat== [[Image:Iwakura mission.jpg|left|250px|thumb|{{ill|Iwakura Mission|en|Iwakura Mission|qid=Q696860|lt=Iwakura Mission|1=Iwakura Mission}}. The head of the mission was Iwakura Tomomi, shown in the picture wearing traditional Japanese clothing]] After the establishment of the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|qid=Q1322181|lt=Government of Meiji Japan|1=Government of Meiji Japan}}, Iwakura played an important role due to the influence and trust he had with {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}}. He put forward the emperor's directly ruled government. He was largely responsible for the promulgation of the [[:en:Five Charter Oath|Five Charter Oath]] of 1868, and the subject {{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|qid=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system|1=Abolition of the han system}}. Also, he established ''[[Daijō-kan|Dajō-kan]]''. Moreover, he planned to move the emperor from Kyoto to Tokyo to make a new political environment. Some court nobles in Kyoto criticized these reforms, but he promoted these restorations with [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] despite their opposition. Soon after his appointment as [[:en:Udaijin|Minister of the Right]] in 1871, he led the two-year around-the-world journey known as the {{ill|Iwakura Mission|en|Iwakura Mission|qid=Q696860|lt=Iwakura Mission|1=Iwakura Mission}}, visiting the {{ill|United States|en|United States|qid=Q30|lt=United States|1=United States}} and several countries in {{ill|Europe|en|Europe|qid=Q46|lt=Europe|1=Europe}} with the purpose of renegotiating the {{ill|Unequal treaties|en|Unequal treaties|qid=Q864737|lt=Unequal treaties|1=Unequal treaties}} and gathering information to help effect the modernization of Japan. American newspapers reported that a visit by Iwakura was as important as a visit by the Prime Minister of the United Kingdom. He failed when he discussed the revision of the unequal treaty with the U.S. president. The U.S. president pointed out that Iwakura did not have a commission of full powers. {{ill|Ōkubo Toshimichi|en|Ōkubo Toshimichi|qid=Q356118|lt=Ōkubo|1=Ōkubo Toshimichi}} and [[Itō Hirobumi|Itō]] immediately returned to Japan on March 24 to get a commission of full powers and then returned to the United States on June 17. Despite their efforts, the United States was no longer interested in the revision of the unequal treaty at that time. During his travels, Iwakura was surprised at the economic prosperity he observed in western countries. He was especially interested in the railways. Also, he learned the importance of religious problems in the United States and he thought that the prohibition of Christianity was an obstacle to change the unequal treaties. Besides, he felt the danger of rapid Westernization, because he saw and learned about the merits and demerits of Westernization. At first, this trip was planned for 10 months and a half, but it took a year and 10 months in the end. A celebration was held in Manchester and Liverpool in 1997 to celebrate the 125th anniversary of the Iwakura Mission. Before the return of the Iwakura mission, the dispatch of [[Saigō Takamori|Saigō]] to Korea was decided in a cabinet meeting. Generally, people thought that the dispatch of Saigō was too dangerous, but Saigō persisted in his decision. On his return to Japan in 1873, he was just in time to prevent an invasion of {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|qid=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}} (''{{ill|Seikanron|en|Seikanron|qid=Q370290|lt=Seikanron|1=Seikanron}}''). Iwakura opposed the dispatch because Japan also faced foreign issues concerning Karafuto and Taiwan. Also, Iwakura wanted to prevent the dispatch of Saigō to avoid foreign wars. Realizing that Japan was not in any position to challenge the western powers in its present state, he advocated strengthening the imperial institution, which he felt could be accomplished through a written {{ill|Constitution|en|Constitution|qid=Q7755|lt=Constitution|1=Constitution}} and a limited form of {{ill|parliamentary democracy|en|parliamentary democracy|qid=Q3043547|lt=parliamentary democracy|1=Parliamentary democracy}}. Ōkubo supported Iwakura's idea. In the next meeting, Saigō was absent to imply his disposal in case his claim would not be accepted. [[Sanjō Sanetomi]], a prime minister was surprised about it and suddenly changed his opinion from the opposition of dispatch to the acceptance of dispatch. A lot of members including Iwakura criticized the sudden mind change of Sanjō, and they requested their disposal. Sanjō panicked and he became unable to work because he wanted to avoid every member's disposal. Instead of Sanjō, Iwakura became the substitute of the prime minister and prevented the dispatch of Saigō. Due to this decision, some people had complaints, and Iwakura was attacked by nine people led by [[Takechi Kumakichi]] in Kuichigaizaka, {{ill|Akasaka, Tokyo|en|Akasaka, Tokyo|qid=Q416131|lt=Akasaka|1=Akasaka, Tokyo}}. He was slightly injured but severely damaged mentally. (Kuichigai Incident) [[File:IWAKURA Tomomi.jpg|250px|thumb|Iwakura c. 1880s]] In 1873, Kido and Ōkubo raised written opinions about a constitution. Both opinions said that Japan should make a constitution as soon as possible, so to establishing a constitution became an urgent issue in Japan. In this situation, Ōkubo was assassinated in 1878, and Iwakura had to choose Itō Hirobumi or Ōkuma Shigenobu as a successor of Ōkubo. Itō wanted an {{ill|Absolute monarchy|en|Absolute monarchy|qid=Q184558|lt=Absolute monarchy|1=Absolute monarchy}} as in Germany while Ōkuma favored a {{ill|Constitutional monarchy|en|Constitutional monarchy|qid=Q41614|lt=Constitutional monarchy|1=Constitutional monarchy}} as exemplified by the United Kingdom. Ultimately, Iwakura chose Itō and the German model as the basis for Japan's constitution. On 19 April 1876, Iwakura became the director of the Peerage Hall. However, in the early Meiji period, it was not clear what exactly they were doing, and as a result they were more independent than they were later on, and conflicts often occurred, especially between those from feudal lords and those from court nobles. It was like that. However, the nobility that Iwakura had in mind was a European-style aristocracy whose mission was solely to support the imperial family. Therefore, it was necessary to make all Chinese people understand that petty conflicts based on the old framework were pointless. However, from around 1880 (Meiji 13), the Freedom and People's Rights Movement gained momentum, and discussions on establishing a constitution accelerated. He ordered {{ill|Inoue Kowashi|en|Inoue Kowashi|qid=Q711131|lt=Inoue Kowashi|1=Inoue Kowashi}} to begin work on a constitution in late June 1881, and ordered [[Itō Hirobumi]] to Europe to study various European systems. In March 1882, Itō departed for Europe. Iwakura had high expectations for the investigation of Itō. On the other hand, during the dispatch of Itō, Iwakura worried that Itō might be really influenced by Germany. Although Iwakura accepted to make a constitution based on the Germany system, he wanted to make a constitution unique to Japan. Also, Iwakura wanted to cherish the Japanese tradition and emperor system. From such an idea, in 1882, He established an Internal regulation interrogation station to investigate imperial ceremonies and Japanese tradition, and offered the install of the National history compilation stations in 1883. This station aimed to translate Japanese history into English. ==Death== [[File:Emperor Visiting Sick Iwakura by Kita Renzo (Meiji Memorial Picture Gallery).jpg|thumb|Emperor Meiji visiting a Sick Iwakura by Kita Renzō]] However, Iwakura himself was unable to witness Ito Hirobumi's return to Japan or the enactment of the [[:en:Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Constitution of the Empire of Japan]]. Although in poor health by early 1883, Iwakura went to Kyoto in May to direct efforts to restore and preserve the imperial palace and the buildings of the old city, many of which had been falling into disrepair since the transfer of the capital to Tokyo. Soon however, he became seriously ill and was confined to his bed. The Meiji Emperor sent his personal physician, {{ill|Erwin Bälz|en|Erwin Bälz|qid=Q71149|lt=Erwin Bälz|1=Erwin Bälz}}, to examine Iwakura; Baelz diagnosed advanced [[:en:throat cancer|throat cancer]]. The emperor personally visited his cousin and old friend on July 19, and was moved to tears at his condition. Iwakura died the following day at the age of 57, and was given a state funeral, the first ever given by the imperial government. Just before dying, Iwakura invited Inoue Kaoru over. Bälz and Inoue were with Iwakura until the end of his life. He was buried in Asamadai, Minami-{{ill|Shinagawa|en|Shinagawa|qid=Q233495|lt=Shinagawa|1=Shinagawa}}. == Personal life == Iwakura Tomomi's height was about 160 cm. He was born in poverty, and he lived a simple life. He used to like [[Sake|Japanese sake]] and dRank around 90 ml, three times a day. However, because of his health condition and food restrictions, he could drink western wine only instead of Japanese sake. During his life after retirement, he did not exercise at all, and ate very little to the extent he was compared to a bird. Iwakura was very particular about what to eat, and was fond of eating fish, meat,{{clarify|date=August 2021}} and vegetables, rather than beef and chicken. He also liked the cuisine of Kyoto, and turtle cuisine was one of his favorites. His hobby was {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|qid=Q226887|lt=Noh dance|1=Noh}} in plain clothes. He was an open-minded person who put himself in the other person's shoes before forming any opinion.{{Cite book|last=Tokutomi|first=Sohō|title=Iwakura Tomomi kō[岩倉具視公]|publisher=Minyū sha|year=1932|location=Tokyo|pages=279–293}} He had a wife, named Mineko, but she died on October 24, 1874. After Mineko's death, he remarried Makiko Noguchi. He is an ancestor to actor {{ill|Ken Uehara|en|Ken Uehara|qid=Q3195097|lt=Ken Uehara|1=Ken Uehara}}, singer {{ill|Yūzō Kayama|en|Yūzō Kayama|qid=Q3113502|lt=Yūzō Kayama|1=Yūzō Kayama}}, and actress {{ill|Emi Ikehata|en|Emi Ikehata|qid=Q3052190|lt=Emi Ikehata|1=Emi Ikehata}}. == Tributes == {{Confusing|section|date=August 2021}} {{ill|Katsu Kaishū|en|Katsu Kaishū|qid=Q700470|lt=Katsu Kaishū|1=Katsu Kaishū}} "Iwakura was open-minded, and he was unique in court nobles." {{ill|Tokutomi Sohō|en|Tokutomi Sohō|qid=Q708794|lt=Tokutomi Sohō|1=Tokutomi Sohō}} "Iwakura was an outstanding person. His appearance from ''{{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|qid=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}}'' to the early Meiji period was lucky for the Imperial Household, the state, and citizens." "Iwakura was a brave man not only in court nobles but also in politicians."{{Cite book|last=Ōtsuka|first=Katsura|title=Meijikokka to Iwakura Tomomi [明治国家と岩倉具視]|publisher=Shinzansha|year=2004|location=Tokyo}} [[Itō Hirobumi]] "Iwakura was like a shelf." (It means that when Itō faced the difficult problem, he could consult Iwakura.){{Cite book|last=Takekoshi|first=Yosaburō|title=Tōankō[陶庵公]|publisher=Sōbunkaku|year=1930|location=Tokyo}} ==Honours== [[File:Series C 500 Yen Bank of Japan note - front.jpg|thumb|250px|right|On the {{ill|Banknotes of the Japanese yen|en|Banknotes of the Japanese yen|qid=Q839051|lt=old 500 yen|1=Banknotes of the Japanese yen}} note]] ''From the corresponding article in the Japanese Wikipedia'' *Grand Cordon of the {{ill|Order of the Rising Sun|en|Order of the Rising Sun|qid=Q818600|lt=Order of the Rising Sun|1=Order of the Rising Sun}} (29 December 1876) *Grand Cordon of the {{ill|Order of the Chrysanthemum|en|Order of the Chrysanthemum|qid=Q696246|lt=Order of the Chrysanthemum|1=Order of the Chrysanthemum}} (1 November 1882) ===Order of precedence=== *Junior fifth Rank (28th day, 10th month of the ninth year of Tenpo (1838)) *Fifth Rank (Fourth day, sixth month of the 12th year of Tenpo (1841)) *Senior fifth Rank (18th day, second month of the second year of Koka (1845)) *Fourth Rank (10th day, sixth month of the seventh year of Ansei (1854)) *Senior fourth Rank (Fifth day, first month of the second year of Man'en (1861)) *Third Rank (Second day, second month of the fourth year of Keio (1868)) *Senior second Rank (25th day, first month of the second year of Meiji (1869)) *First Rank (18 May 1876) *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior first Rank]] (20 July 1885; posthumous) ==Ancestry== {{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e5%b2%a9%e5%80%89%e6%b0%8f%ef%bc%88%e7%be%bd%e6%9e%97%e5%ae%b6%ef%bc%89#tomomi883|title=Iwakura genealogy|website=Reichsarchiv|date=6 May 2010 |access-date=11 September 2017|language=ja}} {{ahnentafel |collapsed=yes |align=center |boxstyle_1=background-color: #fcc; |boxstyle_2=background-color: #fb9; |boxstyle_3=background-color: #ffc; |boxstyle_4=background-color: #bfc; |1= 1. '''Iwakura Tomomi (1825–1883)''' |2= 2. Horikawa Yasuchika (1797–1859) |3= 3. Kanshūji Yoshiko (1799–1861) |4= 4. Hagiwara Kazumiki (1740–1828) |5= |6= 6. Kanshūji Tsunehaya (1748–1805) |7= |8= 8. Hagiwara Kazurei (1718–1784) |9= 9. Kurahashi |10= |11= |12= 12. Kanshūji Akimichi (1717–1756) |13= 13. Inaba |14= |15= }} ==Notes== {{reflist}} == References == * {{ill|William G. ''Japan in Transition: from Tokugawa to Meiji''. New York: Palgrave Macmillan. {{ISBN|9780312239145}}; {{ISBN|9780312239152}}; {{OCLC|45172740}} ==External links== {{Commons+cat|Iwakura Tomomi}} *[http://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/e/datas/23.html?c=1 National Diet bio & portrait] *[http://www.wdl.org/en/item/7368 Meiji Dignitaries] is a portrait of Tomomi and others from 1877 {{Authority control}} [[en:Iwakura Tomomi]] [[ar:إيواكورا تومومي]] [[ca:Iwakura Tomomi]] {{Commons category|Iwakura Tomomi}} [[cs:Tomomi Iwakura]] [[de:Iwakura Tomomi]] [[es:Iwakura Tomomi]] [[fa:ایواکورا تومومی]] [[fr:Iwakura Tomomi]] [[id:Iwakura Tomomi]] [[it:Iwakura Tomomi]] [[ja:岩倉具視]] [[ko:이와쿠라 도모미]] [[nl:Iwakura Tomomi]] [[pl:Tomomi Iwakura]] [[pt:Iwakura Tomomi]] [[ru:Ивакура Томоми]] [[sr:Ивакура Томоми]] [[sv:Iwakura Tomomi]] [[th:อิวากูระ โทโมมิ]] [[tr:Iwakura Tomomi]] [[uk:Івакура Томомі]] [[vi:Iwakura Tomomi]] [[zh:岩倉具視]] [[zh-classical:岩倉具視]] {{wikidata link|Q428200}} [[Category:1825 births]] [[Category:1883 deaths]] [[Category:Japanese politicians]] [[Category:Kazoku]] [[Category:Members of the Iwakura Mission]] [[Category:Nobles of the Meiji Restoration]] [[Category:People from Kyoto]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
